request.question: Rock 'n' Roll Music was an American compilation of album tracks, including the 1965 album opener, with cover versions released by which iconic band?

title: Rock 'n' Roll Singer
I Hate Rock 'n' Roll
Just... Fabulous Rock 'n' Roll
Drive My Car
Rock 'n' Roll (John Lennon album)
Rock 'n' Roll Music (album)
World Rock'n'Roll Confederation
What's Next to the Moon (album)
It's Only Rock 'n Roll
Iowa Rock 'n' Roll Hall of Fame
sentences: Rock 'n' Roll Singer is the debut solo EP from Mark Kozelek.

The EP was released on June 13, 2000, and was released while Kozelek's final album with his previous band Red House Painters (the 1998 album "Old Ramon", which didn't get a release until 2001) was in limbo with record label mergers.

"Rock 'n' Roll Singer" contains three original tracks and four covers: "Rock 'n' Roll Singer," "You Ain't Got a Hold on Me," and "Bad Boy Boogie" originally by AC/DC, and "Around and Around" originally by John Denver.
"I Hate Rock 'n' Roll" is a song by the Scottish alternative rock group The Jesus and Mary Chain.
It was the first single from the band's compilation album, "Hate Rock 'n' Roll".
It was released together with 3 other tracks by Blanco y Negro Records in June 1995 and reached #61 in the UK single charts.
This release was the last release for the band on this label.
The 10" format was numbered and limited to 5000 copies.
The reworked version of the track, which was dubbed as "I Love Rock 'n' Roll" was also included on the group's last album "Munki", released in 1998.
Just... Fabulous Rock 'n' Roll is a studio album by Cliff Richard, released 11 November 2016.
The album continues the rock 'n' roll theme of his previous studio album "The Fabulous Rock 'n' Roll Songbook".
It comprises covers of 14 classic rock 'n' roll songs and one new song "It's Better to Dream".
It features Elvis Presley in duet with Richard in "Blue Suede Shoes" and Peter Frampton on guitar in "Dimples".
The album reached number 4 on the UK Albums Chart and has been certified Gold for sales over 100,000 in the UK.
"Drive My Car" is a song by the Beatles, written primarily by Paul McCartney, with lyrical contributions from John Lennon.
It was first released on the British version of the band's 1965 album "Rubber Soul"; it also appeared in North America on the "Yesterday and Today" collection.
The upbeat, lighthearted "Drive My Car" was used as the opening track for both albums.
Over the years the song has been covered by many artists such as former Spice Girls member Melanie C and Lulu, and the US band Breakfast Club.
which was featured in the 1988 film "License to Drive", that was played over the opening credits.
Rock 'n' Roll is the sixth studio album by John Lennon.
Released in 1975, it is an album of late 1950s and early 1960s songs as covered by Lennon.

Recording the album was problematic and spanned an entire year: Phil Spector produced sessions in October 1973 at A&M Studios, and Lennon produced sessions in October 1974 at Record Plant Studios (East).
Lennon was being sued by Morris Levy over copyright infringement of one line in his song "Come Together".
As part of an agreement, Lennon had to include three Levy-owned songs on "Rock 'n' Roll".
Spector disappeared with the session recordings and was subsequently involved in a motor accident, leaving the album's tracks unrecoverable until the beginning of the "Walls and Bridges" sessions.
With "Walls and Bridges" coming out first, featuring one Levy-owned song, Levy sued Lennon expecting to see Lennon's "Rock 'n' Roll" album.
Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles that consists of previously released Beatles tracks.
The double album was issued on 7 June 1976 in the United States, on Capitol Records (catalogue number SKBO 11537), and on Parlophone (PCSP 719) in the United Kingdom, four days later.

The album is a combination of some notable Lennon–McCartney originals, such as "Drive My Car", "Revolution", "Back in the U.S.S.R." and "Get Back", George Harrison's "Taxman", and a dozen cover versions of songs written by significant rock and roll composers of the 1950s, including Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Carl Perkins and Larry Williams.
"Rock 'n' Roll Music" was the first Beatles album to include "I'm Down", which had previously only been available as the B-side of the "Help!
" single.
World Rock 'n' Roll Confederation (WRRC) was registered in 1984, although its history traces to 1974.
It is an umbrella organization for national professional and amateur Rock and Roll dancesport federations.

Its statute that it "aims at promoting the physical training of its members by means of sporting activities in the form of Rock 'n' Roll dance tournaments, including the acrobatic variations (acrobatic rock 'n' roll) as well as Rock 'n' Roll and Boogie Woogie, Lindy Hop, Formation and alternative styles in line with the rules and sporting presentations".
The registered office is in Ljubljana, Slovenia.
What's Next to the Moon is a solo album by Mark Kozelek, released on January 10, 2001.
The album is composed entirely of drastically rearranged acoustic covers of Bon Scott-era AC/DC songs.
The album follows the release of his debut solo EP "Rock 'n' Roll Singer", which also features three AC/DC covers: "Rock 'n' Roll Singer," "You Ain't Got a Hold on Me," and "Bad Boy Boogie."
The versions of "Rock 'n' Roll Singer" and "Bad Boy Boogie" on this album are different from the versions on the EP.
It's Only Rock 'n Roll is the 12th British and 14th American studio album by The Rolling Stones, released in 1974.
It was the last Rolling Stones album for guitarist Mick Taylor and the songwriting and recording of the album's title track had a connection to Taylor's eventual replacement, Ronnie Wood.
The album also marked the 10th anniversary since the release of the band's debut album, "The Rolling Stones".
"It's Only Rock 'n Roll" has a firmer rock sound than the band's previous album, the more funk - and soul - inspired "Goats Head Soup".
"It's Only Rock 'n Roll" reached #1 in the US and #2 in the UK.
The Iowa Rock ‘n’ Roll Hall of Fame is a museum located in Arnolds Park, Iowa and maintained by the non-profit Iowa Rock ‘n’ Roll Music Association (IRRMA).
The mission of IRRMA is to “retain and honor the legacy of rock ’n’ roll music and preserve the history of music in the state of Iowa.”

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Rock 'n' Roll Music was an American compilation of album tracks, including the 1965 album opener, with cover versions released by which iconic band?
hop1_docs: The Pretty Things (album) | The Pretty Things is the self-titled 1965 release by the English rock band The Pretty Things, which features mostly R&B and rock and roll cover versions.  A re-issue released in 2000 on CD featured every track from both the American and British versions of the album.

I Hate Rock 'n' Roll | "I Hate Rock 'n' Roll" is a song by the Scottish alternative rock group The Jesus and Mary Chain.  It was the first single from the band's compilation album, "Hate Rock 'n' Roll".  It was released together with 3 other tracks by Blanco y Negro Records in June 1995 and reached #61 in the UK single charts.  This release was the last release for the band on this label.  The 10" format was numbered and limited to 5000 copies.  The reworked version of the track, which was dubbed as "I Love Rock 'n' Roll" was also included on the group's last album "Munki", released in 1998.

Rock 'n' Roll Music (album) | Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles that consists of previously released Beatles tracks.  The double album was issued on 7 June 1976 in the United States, on Capitol Records (catalogue number SKBO 11537), and on Parlophone (PCSP 719) in the United Kingdom, four days later.  The album is a combination of some notable Lennon–McCartney originals, such as "Drive My Car", "Revolution", "Back in the U.S.S.R." and "Get Back", George Harrison's "Taxman", and a dozen cover versions of songs written by significant rock and roll composers of the 1950s, including Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Carl Perkins and Larry Williams.  "Rock 'n' Roll Music" was the first Beatles album to include "I'm Down", which had previously only been available as the B-side of the "Help! " single.

Banquet (album) | Banquet is the fourth album by German progressive rock band Lucifer's Friend, released in 1974.  By this time there was no hard rock sound, it was now strictly progressive rock mixed with jazz fusion.  The song "Our World Is a Rock 'n' Roll Band" was the opening track on some versions (i.e. the U.S. version) of the original LP release, but the only CD releases to date do not include this track, which can instead be found as a bonus track on the Repertoire Records CD release of their self-titled debut.

Ultimate Hits: Rock and Roll Never Forgets | Ultimate Hits: Rock and Roll Never Forgets is a compilation album by American rock singer–songwriter Bob Seger.  The double-disc album was released on November 21, 2011 and contains 26 remastered tracks from throughout Seger's career, which spans more than four decades.  Included are the original mono version of "Ramblin' Gamblin' Man", Seger's first hit with The Bob Seger System from 1968, the classic Christmas song "The Little Drummer Boy" from 1987's "A Very Special Christmas", which makes its first appearance on a Seger album, and previously unreleased cover versions of Tom Waits' "Downtown Train" and Little Richard's "Hey, Hey, Hey, Hey (Going Back to Birmingham)."  There is also a Walmart exclusive edition that includes the bonus track "Living Inside My Heart," a song from the soundtrack of the 1986 film "About Last Night...", which has also never before been released on any Bob Seger album.  Two songs on this compilation album are edited compared to the original releases: "We've Got Tonight" is the single edit, which is about one minute shorter than the album version, and "Katmandu" is a newly edited version which omits the second verse, making the song also about one minute shorter compared to the original album version.  In the US it was certified gold and platinum in June 2013 by the RIAA.

His Best (Bo Diddley album) | His Best is a 1997 greatest hits compilation album by American rock and roll icon Bo Diddley released by Chess and MCA Records on April 8, 1997 (see 1997 in music).  The album was re-released by Geffen Records on April 17, 2007 as The Definitive Collection with a different album cover.  "The Definitive Collection" reached #2 on "Billboard" magazine's Blues Albums chart on June 21, 2008, which was the week that the album debuted on the charts.

Icon (Blink-182 album) | Icon is a compilation album from the American rock band Blink-182, released on March 29, 2013.  "Icon" was released following the band's departure from Interscope Records in 2012 and compiles the band's biggest singles, covering material from "Dude Ranch" (1997) to "Blink-182" (2003).
hop1_titles: Ultimate Hits: Rock and Roll Never Forgets
response.summary_1:
Topic: Beatles' Rock 'n' Roll Music compilation and 1965 album (The Pretty Things).

Facts supported by passages:
- "Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles." (passage 3)
- It was "issued on 7 June 1976 in the United States" (passage 3).
- The album contains notable Lennon–McCartney originals: "Drive My Car", "Revolution", "Back in the U.S.S.R." and "Get Back." (passage 3)
- It includes "a dozen cover versions ... including Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Carl Perkins and Larry Williams." (passage 3)
- "'Rock 'n' Roll Music' was the first Beatles album to include 'I'm Down' ... previously only been available as the B-side of the 'Help!' single." (passage 3)
- "The Pretty Things is the self-titled 1965 release" and "features mostly R&B and rock and roll cover versions." (passage 1)

Ambiguities / missing facts blocking answer:
- Missing fact: the identity and title of the "1965 album opener" referenced in the question (not given in passages). Bridge entity needed: the specific 1965 album and its opening track.
- Ambiguity: whether the question's "1965 album opener" refers to The Pretty Things (1965) opener or to a 1965 Beatles track (e.g., "I'm Down"); passages do not link that opener to the Rock 'n' Roll Music compilation explicitly.

Prioritized next-hop retrievals (in order):
1. "Rock 'n' Roll Music (album)" — inspect Lede/Infobox and full Track listing section to see which 1965 opener track(s) are included and which cover versions are credited to which artist.
2. "The Pretty Things (album)" — inspect Lede and Track listing (US vs UK versions) to identify the 1965 album opener title and whether it appears on Beatles' Rock 'n' Roll Music or was covered by another band.
3. Search query / page: "I'm Down (Beatles) '1965' 'album opener' 'cover version'" — check the I'm Down article or cover-version lists to see who released cover versions.
4. If needed: searches for specific composer names listed ("Chuck Berry", "Little Richard", "Carl Perkins", "Larry Williams") combined with "cover" + "Beatles Rock 'n' Roll Music track listing".
response.hop2_query: Which band released cover versions of The Pretty Things 1965 album opener
hop2_docs: The Pretty Things (album) | The Pretty Things is the self-titled 1965 release by the English rock band The Pretty Things, which features mostly R&B and rock and roll cover versions.  A re-issue released in 2000 on CD featured every track from both the American and British versions of the album.

Wondering (Dirty Pretty Things song) | "Wondering" is a song by the band Dirty Pretty Things.  It was released as a single on 15 October 2006 and was the third to be released from the band's debut album "Waterloo to Anywhere".  Early versions of the song generally sported the title "If You Were Wondering", the single-word title being settled upon for the final release of "Waterloo to Anywhere".
Get the Picture? (The Pretty Things album) | Get the Picture?  is the second album by the English rock group The Pretty Things, released in 1965.

Don't Bring Me Down (The Pretty Things song) | "Don't Bring Me Down" is a song written by Johnny Dee (road manager for British band The Fairies) and first performed by the rock band The Pretty Things in 1964.  It was a number 10 hit on the UK Singles Chart for them, and reached number 34 in Canada.  The song was featured on the American version of their debut album, "The Pretty Things".

The Pretty Things/Philippe DeBarge | The Pretty Things/Philippe DeBarge is an album recorded by the English rock band the Pretty Things with French playboy Philippe DeBarge in September 1969, but not released until 2009.
Balboa Island (The Pretty Things album) | Balboa Island, released in 2007, is the eleventh studio album by the English rock band The Pretty Things.

Parachute (The Pretty Things album) | Parachute, released in 1970, is the fifth studio album by the English rock band The Pretty Things, following "S.F. Sorrow" and preceding "Freeway Madness".  It is their first album without guitarist Dick Taylor.
hop2_titles: Get the Picture? (The Pretty Things album)
Don't Bring Me Down (The Pretty Things song)
response.summary_2: Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles that collects Lennon–McCartney originals and a dozen cover versions. The Pretty Things is the self-titled 1965 release and features mostly R&B and rock and roll cover versions.
response.answer: I can’t determine the band from the provided summaries — they don’t say which band released cover versions of the 1965 album opener. Please provide the opener’s title or allow me to check the full Rock 'n' Roll Music / The Pretty Things pages so I can look up which band recorded the cover.
